name: Ebro Natural Trail - Aragón (GR 99)
name_original: Galachos de Juslibol — por el Camino Natural del Ebro - GR 99
description: Hike a moderate 6.3-mile (10.2 km) section of the Ebro Natural Trail (GR 99) through Galacho de Juslibol, offering river views and diverse l
region_intro: Easy hiking trails around Zaragoza offer access to diverse natural landscapes, from riverine ecosystems along the Ebro River to semi-arid steppes and chalky hills. The region features "sotos" (riverside forests) and "galachos" (isolated river meanders), providing tranquil settings for walks. While the city is urban, its surroundings include distinct environments like the Montes Blancos de Alfajarín, characterized by unique geological formations. These trails provide opportunities to explore varied terrain within easy reach of the city. Best easy hiking trails around Zaragoza The most popular easy hiking route is Historic center of Zaragoza — circular from Av. de Cataluña, a 4.2 miles (6.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 47 minutes to complete. This route explores the city's historical landmarks and urban green spaces. Another top favourite among local hikers is Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond — circular through the Pinares de la Alfranca, an easy 4.3 miles (6.9 km) path. This trail winds through gardens, past a pond, and into pine forests. Local hikers also love the Almonacid de la Cuba-Belchite Trail, a 4.2 miles (6.7 km) trail leading through rural landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 47 minutes. Hiking around Zaragoza is defined by riverine ecosystems, semi-arid steppes, and chalky hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, with a focus on easy trails in this guide. The routes in Zaragoza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. More than 3,700 hikers have used komoot to explore Zaragoza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many easy hiking trails are available around Zaragoza?
A: Zaragoza offers a wide selection of easy hiking trails, with over 170 routes specifically categorized as easy. In total, the region boasts more than 420 hiking options for various skill levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on easy hikes near Zaragoza?
A: Easy hikes around Zaragoza showcase diverse landscapes. You'll find tranquil riverine ecosystems along the Ebro River, featuring 'sotos' (riverside forests) and 'galachos' (isolated river meanders) like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10467724" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galachos of Juslibol&lt;/a&gt;. The region also includes unique semi-arid steppes and chalky hills, such as those found around the Montes Blancos de Alfajarín, offering distinct geological formations.

Q: What do other hikers say about the easy trails in Zaragoza?
A: The easy trails in Zaragoza are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. Hikers often praise the accessibility to diverse natural environments and the variety of routes available within easy reach of the city.

Q: Are there any easy circular walks around Zaragoza?
A: Yes, many easy trails around Zaragoza are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. Popular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974401312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic center of Zaragoza — circular from Av. de Cataluña&lt;/a&gt;, which explores urban landmarks, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974939209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond — circular through the Pinares de la Alfranca&lt;/a&gt;, winding through gardens and pine forests.

Q: Can I find easy trails suitable for families in Zaragoza?
A: Absolutely. Many easy trails in Zaragoza are perfect for families, offering gentle terrain and opportunities to explore nature. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974939209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond&lt;/a&gt; is a great example, providing a pleasant walk through varied scenery. The Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park, though a bit further out, also offers an easy 2.4-mile trail suitable for all fitness levels.

Q: Are there dog-friendly easy hiking options in Zaragoza?
A: While specific dog regulations can vary by park or natural area, many easy trails around Zaragoza, especially those in more open or rural settings, are generally dog-friendly. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash and clean up after them. Trails along the Ebro River or through the 'galachos' often provide good opportunities for walks with your canine companion.

Q: Are there easy hikes accessible by public transport in Zaragoza?
A: Many easy hikes in and around Zaragoza are accessible via public transport, particularly those closer to the city center or in well-connected areas. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974401312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic center of Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; or those along the Imperial Canal of Aragón are often reachable by urban bus lines. For trails slightly further out, regional buses might be an option, but checking local transport schedules is advised.

Q: Where can I find parking for easy hikes near Zaragoza?
A: For trails within Zaragoza city limits, public parking garages or street parking (often paid) are available. For routes slightly outside the city, such as those in natural parks or rural areas, you can usually find designated parking lots at trailheads or near popular access points. It's best to check specific route details or local signage for parking information.

Q: Are there any easy trails with waterfalls or unique water features?
A: Yes, the region offers some beautiful water features. While not directly on an easy trail listed in this guide, the Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park is famous for its stunning waterfalls and verdant forests, offering a picturesque experience. Closer to Zaragoza, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e976108152" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Dam of Almonacid de la Cuba and Pool of Los Chorros&lt;/a&gt; offers an easy hike to a unique water attraction. The 'Aguallueve de Anento,' known for its continuously seeping water, is another interesting natural formation in the wider region.

Q: What are some interesting natural features or landmarks to see on easy hikes?
A: Easy hikes around Zaragoza offer glimpses of various natural features and landmarks. You can explore the unique 'galachos' (old river meanders) like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5238060" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balsa de Larralde&lt;/a&gt;, or discover viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/94970" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de las Planas&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979614617" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro de Osborne and Castillo de Cornel trail&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the distinctive Montes Blancos de Alfajarín, offering views of the iconic Toro de Osborne sign and the historic Castillo de Cornel.

Q: What is the best time of year for easy hiking in Zaragoza?
A: Spring (March to May) and Autumn (September to November) are generally the most pleasant seasons for easy hiking in Zaragoza, offering mild temperatures and comfortable conditions. Summers can be very hot, especially in the semi-arid areas, so early mornings or late evenings are preferable. Winters are typically mild, making hiking possible year-round, though some trails might be muddy after rain.

Q: Are there any easy routes that explore the city's history or urban green spaces?
A: Yes, you can easily combine history and nature within the city. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974401312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic center of Zaragoza — circular from Av. de Cataluña&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent easy route that explores the city's historical landmarks and urban green spaces. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1397705" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zaragoza Central Market – Stone Bridge loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a pleasant walk through the city's heart.
tips: Escarpe viewpoint located in the province of Zaragoza with beautiful views of the Ebro valley.

The Castle of Miranda is located approximately 10 kilometers from Zaragoza, in the heart of the Castellar mountainous area, above the Juslibol galachos. It is believed that it was built in the 12th century, but before that there was already an Iberian settlement there, of which today only some of the plants of their homes remain. The castle, now in ruins, has never been restored or preserved, but was of great importance in its day. From there, King Alfonso I 'El Batallador' created the strategy to reconquer Zaragoza (in 1118) and consolidated the positions of Juslibol and El Castellar, back in 1105.
Idyllic place to observe the Ebro river near the Galacho de Juslibol, so, without a doubt, we can add it to our route the day we pass through the Galacho.
Extraordinary place to play sports and enjoy the native flora and fauna. There are picnic sites and different paths for walking or cycling. On weekends it can be a bit crowded, so BEWARE!

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike this section of the Ebro Natural Trail?
faqs.answer: This specific komoot tour, covering approximately 10.16 km, typically takes around 2 hours and 42 minutes to complete. This duration is for the 'hike' sport type and can vary based on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this trail section?
faqs.answer: This particular section of the Ebro Natural Trail is rated as moderate. While the overall GR 99 can be challenging, this route is suitable for hikers with good fitness, offering accessible paths within the broader Aragonese landscape.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights and viewpoints along this route?
faqs.answer: Along this route, you'll encounter several notable spots. Key highlights include the Escarpe Viewpoint, offering panoramic views, and a View of Castillo de Miranda. You'll also get a beautiful View of the Ebro River itself, and pass through the unique natural area of the Galachos of Juslibol.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this section of the Ebro Natural Trail?
faqs.answer: This section of the trail, particularly around the Galachos de Juslibol, features varied terrain. You can expect paths through riverside groves and areas where the Ebro forms meanders. The broader Aragonese stretch of the GR 99 includes gypsiferous and saltpetre lands characteristic of the Ebro depression's steppe environment, though this specific route is likely to be more focused on the riverine landscape.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The Ebro Natural Trail in Aragón is generally best enjoyed in spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot in the Ebro valley, and winters can be chilly, though often dry. These shoulder seasons offer pleasant hiking conditions and vibrant natural scenery.

faqs.question: Is this section of the Ebro Natural Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this section of the Ebro Natural Trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in natural areas like the Galachos de Juslibol, to protect local wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Be sure to carry water for your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail or the Galachos de Juslibol?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this section of the Ebro Natural Trail or to access the Galachos de Juslibol. It is a publicly accessible natural area. However, always respect local regulations and conservation efforts within the natural park.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this trail?
faqs.answer: Access to this section of the trail, particularly for the Galachos de Juslibol area, is typically from the outskirts of Zaragoza. There are usually designated parking areas near the access points to the Galachos, often on the city side of the Ebro River, from where you can begin your hike.

faqs.question: Does this section of the Ebro Natural Trail connect with other paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is part of the extensive Camino natural del Ebro - Aragón (GR 99). Within this specific section, it also passes through parts of the Camino de los Galachos, Camino antiguo de Alfocea, Sendero del Agua, and Sendero del Bosque, offering opportunities to explore interconnected local trails.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife or plants might I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The Ebro valley, especially around the Galachos de Juslibol, is rich in biodiversity. You might spot various bird species, particularly water birds, given the riverine environment. The flora includes unique species adapted to the steppe environment of the Ebro depression, such as Russian thistles and white wormwood, alongside lush riverside vegetation.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation near the trail?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself passes through natural areas, its proximity to Zaragoza means that amenities like cafes, restaurants, and accommodation are readily available in the city. Within the immediate vicinity of the Galachos de Juslibol, options might be limited, so it's advisable to plan for refreshments before or after your hike in Zaragoza.
